class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Business Finance and Time Value of Money
learning_objectives: Explain the goal of financial management and the role of the financial manager
Calculate future value and present value of single cash flows
Apply time value of money concepts to business decisions
readings: Time Value of Money Reference Sheet

lecture_notes.introduction: Business finance is about making decisions that maximize the value of the firm. The time value of money is the foundational concept: a dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow.
key_concepts: Goal of the firm: maximize shareholder wealth
Time value of money: compounding and discounting
Future Value = PV x (1 + r)^n
Present Value = FV / (1 + r)^n
The role of interest rates and risk
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating how much you need to invest today at 8% annual return to have $1,000,000 at retirement
setup_requirements: Textbook: Berk & DeMarzo, Corporate Finance (5th ed.)
Financial calculator (TI BA II Plus or equivalent)

review.value: Money available now is worth more than the same amount in the future due to its earning potential
Earning interest on interest; exponential growth over time at rate (1+r)^n

learning_objectives: Calculate present and future values of annuities

lecture_notes.introduction: Most financial decisions involve streams of cash flows, not single payments. Annuities and perpetuities provide elegant formulas for valuing these streams.
key_concepts: Ordinary annuity vs. annuity due
PV of annuity = PMT x [(1 - (1+r)^-n) / r]
PV of perpetuity = PMT / r
Growing perpetuity = PMT / (r - g)
Loan amortization and interest vs. principal allocation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a 30-year mortgage amortization schedule and analyzing total interest paid

review.value: A series of equal cash flows occurring at regular intervals for a finite period
A series of equal cash flows continuing forever; valued as PMT/r

learning_objectives: Value bonds using present value techniques
Explain the relationship between bond prices and interest rates
Interpret yield to maturity and the yield curve

lecture_notes.introduction: Bonds are the primary way companies and governments borrow money. Understanding bond valuation is essential for both investors and corporate financial managers.
key_concepts: Bond terminology: face value, coupon rate, maturity, yield
Bond price = PV of coupon payments + PV of face value
Inverse relationship between price and yield
Yield to maturity as internal rate of return
Yield curve shapes and what they signal
lecture_notes.demonstration: Pricing a corporate bond and analyzing how its price changes with interest rate movements

review.value: Sum of PV of all future coupon payments plus PV of par value, discounted at yield to maturity
When rates rise, bond prices fall; longer maturity bonds have greater interest rate sensitivity

learning_objectives: Value stocks using dividend discount and free cash flow models
Explain market efficiency and its implications for investors

lecture_notes.introduction: Stock valuation connects financial theory to real-world investing. Understanding how stock prices are determined helps managers make decisions that create shareholder value.
key_concepts: Dividend discount model: P = D1 / (r - g)
Free cash flow valuation for non-dividend-paying firms
CAPM: E(r) = Rf + beta x (Rm - Rf)
Systematic vs. unsystematic risk
Efficient market hypothesis: weak, semi-strong, strong forms
lecture_notes.demonstration: Valuing a real company's stock using both DDM and comparable company multiples
setup_requirements: Company financial data for valuation exercise

review.value: Stock price equals the present value of all expected future dividends; for constant growth: P = D1/(r-g)
Expected return is the risk-free rate plus a risk premium proportional to the stock's beta (systematic risk)

learning_objectives: Calculate and interpret Net Present Value
Calculate and interpret Internal Rate of Return
Compare NPV, IRR, and payback period methods

lecture_notes.introduction: Capital budgeting is how firms decide which long-term investments to undertake. NPV is the gold standard, but understanding all methods helps managers communicate with diverse stakeholders.
key_concepts: NPV = sum of PV of all cash flows (including initial investment)
NPV rule: accept if NPV > 0
IRR: discount rate that makes NPV = 0
NPV vs. IRR conflicts with mutually exclusive projects
Payback period: simplicity vs. ignoring time value
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating two competing factory expansion projects using NPV, IRR, and payback, highlighting where methods disagree

review.value: Accept projects with positive NPV; they add value to the firm. NPV directly measures wealth creation.
Can give multiple solutions with non-conventional cash flows; may rank mutually exclusive projects incorrectly

class_schedule.topic: Capital Structure and Cost of Capital
learning_objectives: Calculate weighted average cost of capital (WACC)
Analyze the impact of leverage on firm value and risk

lecture_notes.introduction: How a firm finances itself affects its cost of capital and ultimately its value. The WACC is the hurdle rate for investment decisions.
key_concepts: WACC = (E/V) x Re + (D/V) x Rd x (1 - T)
Cost of equity estimation using CAPM
Cost of debt: yield to maturity adjusted for tax shield
Modigliani-Miller without taxes: capital structure irrelevance
With taxes: debt creates a tax shield that adds value
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating WACC for a publicly traded company using market data and evaluating an investment decision

review.value: The blended cost of all capital sources, weighted by their proportion in the firm's capital structure, used as the discount rate for project evaluation
Interest expense is tax-deductible, making debt cheaper on an after-tax basis: effective cost = Rd x (1-T)

quizzes.topic: Time Value of Money and Bond Valuation

questions.question: You want to retire in 30 years with $2,000,000. If you can earn 7% annually, how much must you deposit at the end of each year? Show your calculation.
A 10-year bond with a $1,000 face value pays a 6% annual coupon. If market interest rates rise to 8%, will the bond trade at a premium or discount? Explain the economic intuition.
questions.answer: The bond will trade at a discount (below $1,000). Since the bond pays only 6% while the market demands 8%, investors will not pay full price for below-market coupons. The price must drop until the yield to maturity equals 8%. The discount compensates buyers for receiving lower coupon payments relative to what they could earn on newly issued bonds at current market rates.

questions.question: A project costs $500,000 upfront and generates cash flows of $150,000/year for 5 years. If the required return is 10%, calculate the NPV and state whether the project should be accepted.
Why might NPV and IRR give conflicting rankings for two mutually exclusive projects? Which method should be preferred and why?
questions.answer: Conflicts arise when projects differ in scale (investment size) or cash flow timing. IRR measures a percentage return, which favors smaller projects that earn high rates on less capital. NPV measures absolute dollar value creation. A project with 50% IRR on $10,000 creates less wealth than one with 20% IRR on $1,000,000. NPV should be preferred because it directly measures the increase in firm value, which aligns with the goal of maximizing shareholder wealth.

rubric.key: Identifies causes of conflict (scale or timing)
Explains reasoning linked to shareholder wealth

major_assignments.title: Corporate Financial Analysis and Valuation
major_assignments.description: Select a publicly traded company and perform a comprehensive financial analysis including ratio analysis, WACC calculation, stock valuation using multiple methods, and a capital budgeting analysis of a proposed investment.
objectives: Apply financial analysis techniques to real company data
Calculate and interpret cost of capital
Value a company using multiple approaches
deliverables: Excel workbook with all calculations and models
Stock valuation using DDM and DCF methods
Capital budgeting analysis of a proposed company investment

criteria: Correct calculation of key financial ratios
WACC correctly calculated with sourced inputs
DDM or DCF model properly constructed
Comparison of valuation to market price
Cash flow projections are reasonable and justified
Proper sourcing of all financial data
Thorough financial analysis with correct calculations
Sound capital budgeting analysis and recommendation
Professional presentation with clear data sourcing

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of financial concepts and calculations

problem_sets.description: Weekly quantitative problem sets covering TVM, valuation, and capital budgeting

major_assignments.description: Corporate financial analysis and valuation project

midterm_examination.description: Covers time value of money, bond and stock valuation

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam emphasizing capital budgeting and capital structure

course_policies.attendance: Attendance expected. Financial calculator must be brought to every class session.
course_policies.late_submissions: Problem sets penalized 15% per day. Major project penalized 10% per day with maximum 3-day extension.
course_policies.collaboration: Problem sets may be discussed but must be submitted independently. Valuation project is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All data sources must be cited. Using pre-built valuation models without modification and attribution is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Financial calculator (TI BA II Plus recommended). Excel proficiency required for financial modeling.
